report_metadata.prognostic_context: Sinonasal mucosal melanoma carries a worse prognosis than oral mucosal melanoma, with median OS approximately 20-25 months for localized disease. The KIT exon 11 mutation (distinct from KIT amplification) predicts imatinib sensitivity with response rates up to 54% in KIT-mutant patients versus 0% in amplification-only cases. Five-year OS for sinonasal mucosal melanoma is approximately 34.8% across all stages.

treatments.name: Surgery (Endoscopic Resection) + Adjuvant Radiation Therapy (IMRT/Proton Beam)

evidence_level.rationale: Supported by NCCN Head and Neck guidelines, multiple retrospective cohort studies, systematic reviews, and meta-analyses. No phase 3 RCTs but considered definitive standard of care based on extensive institutional data.

survival_benefit.rationale: Surgery with adjuvant RT yields 2-year OS of 70%, 5-year OS of 46%. Adjuvant RT reduces local recurrence by up to 45% per meta-analysis. Endoscopic resection shows comparable or superior outcomes to open surgery (1-year survival 80% vs 30% in one study). Median OS ~20-25 months for T3 disease.

accessibility.rationale: Widely available at academic medical centers and community hospitals. Endoscopic approach requires experienced skull base surgeon. IMRT is standard; proton beam available at specialized centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Endoscopic approach has lower morbidity than open resection with less blood loss, shorter hospital stay, and faster recovery. Adjuvant RT with IMRT reduces toxicity to surrounding structures. Grade 3 dermatitis and mucositis are possible. Proton beam shows no Grade 3+ late toxicities.

biomarker_match.rationale: Surgery and radiation are universally applicable regardless of molecular profile. No biomarker requirements.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Complete surgical excision removes the primary tumor with negative margins. Adjuvant radiation therapy (65-70 Gy in 30-35 fractions) targets residual microscopic disease in the surgical bed, reducing local recurrence risk. Proton beam therapy offers superior dose conformity sparing critical structures (optic nerves, brain).

key_evidence.study_name: Sinonasal Mucosal Melanoma Meta-Analysis and Adjuvant RT Combined Analysis
key_evidence.journal: International Journal of Radiation Oncology Biology Physics / Journal of Personalized Medicine

treatments.name: Adjuvant Nivolumab (Post-Surgery + RT)

evidence_level.rationale: Adjuvant nivolumab supported by CheckMate-238 data (cutaneous melanoma) extrapolated to mucosal melanoma. Multiple retrospective studies of adjuvant ICI in sinonasal mucosal melanoma show 5-year OS of 42.6%. NCCN recommends considering adjuvant immunotherapy for mucosal melanoma.

survival_benefit.rationale: Adjuvant ICI associated with 5-year OS of 42.6% (95% CI: 39.4-45.8%) versus historical 5-year OS of ~25-30% without adjuvant systemic therapy. 7 of 11 comparative studies demonstrated survival benefit with adjuvant ICI. 1-year OS 86.9%, 2-year OS 74.1%.

accessibility.rationale: Nivolumab is FDA-approved for adjuvant melanoma treatment. Widely available at oncology centers. Covered by insurance for melanoma indication.

safety_profile.rationale: Well-established safety profile. Immune-related adverse events include thyroid dysfunction, fatigue, rash, colitis, hepatitis. Grade 3-4 events occur in approximately 15-20% of patients on monotherapy. Generally manageable with corticosteroids.

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 20% expression suggests moderate likelihood of response. PD-L1 is not a definitive predictor in mucosal melanoma but positive expression is generally favorable. TMB tends to be lower in mucosal melanoma, which may limit response.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Nivolumab is a PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor that blocks the PD-1/PD-L1 interaction, restoring T-cell-mediated anti-tumor immunity. In the adjuvant setting after complete surgical resection, it targets micrometastatic disease to reduce recurrence risk.

key_evidence.study_name: Systematic Review of Adjuvant ICI in Sinonasal Mucosal Melanoma

biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 expression favorable but not required

treatments.name: Imatinib (KIT Exon 11 Mutation-Targeted Therapy)

evidence_level.rationale: Multiple phase II trials demonstrate clear activity in KIT exon 11-mutant mucosal melanoma. Hodi et al. and Carvajal et al. phase II trials provide robust evidence. No phase 3 randomized data but considered standard targeted approach for KIT-mutant disease.

survival_benefit.rationale: KIT-mutant patients: ORR 54%, DCR 77% (vs 0% ORR and 18% DCR for KIT amplification only). KIT exon 11 mutations specifically show 80% metabolic response rate. Median PFS 2.8-4.2 months, median OS 10.7-18.0 months for metastatic disease. Best used as adjuvant or for recurrence.

accessibility.rationale: Imatinib is FDA-approved for GIST and CML with well-established generic availability. Off-label use for KIT-mutant melanoma is recognized by NCCN guidelines and supported by insurance with proper documentation.

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized safety profile from decades of use in GIST/CML. Common side effects include edema, nausea, fatigue, diarrhea, muscle cramps. Generally well-tolerated with dose modifications. Grade 3-4 events less common than with immunotherapy.

biomarker_match.rationale: This patient has a confirmed KIT exon 11 activating mutation, which is the strongest predictor of imatinib response. Exon 11 mutations show 80% metabolic response vs 8% for non-exon 11 mutations. KIT mutation (not amplification) is the critical distinction for imatinib sensitivity.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Imatinib is a small-molecule tyrosine kinase inhibitor that competitively binds the ATP-binding site of KIT, blocking constitutive kinase activation caused by exon 11 mutations. This inhibits downstream RAS-MAPK and PI3K-AKT signaling, suppressing melanoma cell proliferation and survival.

key_evidence.study_name: Multicenter Phase II Trial - Imatinib for KIT-Mutant Melanoma (Carvajal/Hodi)
key_evidence.journal: Journal of Clinical Oncology / Cancer Imaging

biomarker_requirements: KIT exon 11 or exon 13 activating mutation

treatments.name: Nivolumab + Ipilimumab (Dual Checkpoint Blockade)

evidence_level.rationale: Pooled analysis of 86 mucosal melanoma patients from CheckMate clinical trials. 10-year follow-up data from CheckMate-067 supports combination in advanced melanoma. Mucosal melanoma subgroup data consistently shows improved ORR over monotherapy.

survival_benefit.rationale: Combination ORR 37.1% vs 23.3% for nivolumab alone in mucosal melanoma. Median PFS 5.9 months vs 3.0 months for monotherapy. 24-month OS rate of 56%. Some responders achieve durable survival exceeding 56 months. Represents best systemic response rate in advanced mucosal melanoma.

accessibility.rationale: Both agents FDA-approved for advanced melanoma. Widely available at oncology centers. The combination is specifically NCCN-recommended for unresectable/metastatic melanoma.

safety_profile.rationale: Substantially increased toxicity compared to monotherapy. Grade 3-4 immune-related adverse events occur in 55-60% of patients. Colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, and endocrinopathies are significant risks. Treatment discontinuation due to toxicity in ~35-40% of patients.

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 20% expression supports potential responsiveness. Dual checkpoint blockade can overcome some resistance mechanisms seen in low-TMB tumors like mucosal melanoma. Not dependent on specific driver mutations.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Nivolumab (anti-PD-1) blocks the PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitory axis while ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) enhances T-cell priming and activation. The combination produces complementary immune activation: CTLA-4 blockade broadens the T-cell repertoire while PD-1 blockade sustains effector function in the tumor microenvironment.

key_evidence.study_name: Pooled Analysis of CheckMate Trials - Mucosal Melanoma Subgroup

biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 expression favorable but not required

treatments.name: Adjuvant Proton Beam Therapy (Post-Surgery)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II study data and multiple retrospective series support proton beam therapy for sinonasal mucosal melanoma. Takayesu et al. 2025 report adjuvant proton beam data. Carbon ion comparison data available. No phase 3 RCTs comparing proton to photon RT.

survival_benefit.rationale: 1-year local control 88%, 1-year OS 88%, DMFS 75% in adjuvant proton setting. 5-year OS 51% and local control 62% in definitive proton series. Charged particle therapy shows higher 5-year OS and DFS compared to photon IMRT in meta-analysis.

accessibility.rationale: Proton beam therapy is available at approximately 40+ centers in the US. Carbon ion therapy is available at fewer than 15 centers worldwide (primarily Japan, Germany, Italy, China). Referral and travel required for most patients.

safety_profile.rationale: No Grade 3 or higher late toxicities observed in adjuvant proton cohort. No brain necrosis, osteoradionecrosis, or high-grade visual/hearing complications. Acute Grade 3 dermatitis (3/8) and mucositis (2/8) were manageable. Superior conformity reduces dose to optic pathway and brain.

biomarker_match.rationale: Radiation therapy is universally applicable regardless of molecular profile. No biomarker requirements.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Proton beam therapy exploits the Bragg peak phenomenon, delivering maximum radiation dose at a precise depth with rapid dose fall-off beyond the target. This allows escalated dose to the tumor bed while minimizing radiation to adjacent critical structures (optic nerves, chiasm, brain, contralateral orbit). Carbon ion therapy adds higher relative biological effectiveness (RBE) for potentially superior cell kill.

key_evidence.study_name: Adjuvant Proton Beam Radiation Therapy for Sinonasal Mucosal Melanoma

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab Monotherapy

evidence_level.rationale: KEYNOTE-001, KEYNOTE-002, KEYNOTE-006 pooled analysis of 84 mucosal melanoma patients. Well-established phase 3 data in melanoma, with mucosal subgroup analyses available.

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 19% in mucosal melanoma (lower than cutaneous). Median PFS 2.8 months, median OS 11.3 months. Durable responses with median duration 27.6 months in responders. Lower efficacy than combination ipi/nivo but better tolerated.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for advanced melanoma. Widely available at all oncology centers. Well-established insurance coverage and dosing protocols.

safety_profile.rationale: Better tolerated than nivolumab + ipilimumab combination. Grade 3-4 events in approximately 10-15% of patients. Well-characterized immune-related adverse event profile. Can be managed with established protocols.

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 20% provides some predictive value for pembrolizumab response. However, mucosal melanoma has lower TMB than cutaneous melanoma, which may limit checkpoint inhibitor efficacy. PD-L1 is not a definitive predictor in mucosal subtypes.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody targeting PD-1, blocking its interaction with PD-L1 and PD-L2. This releases the immune brake on tumor-specific T cells, enabling anti-tumor cytotoxic responses.

key_evidence.study_name: KEYNOTE Pooled Analysis - Mucosal Melanoma Subgroup

biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 expression favorable but not required

treatments.name: Ripretinib (Next-Generation KIT Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase I expansion cohort with 26 KIT-altered melanoma patients. Encouraging preliminary results. 58% of enrolled patients had mucosal melanoma. No phase 3 data yet.

survival_benefit.rationale: For KIT exon 11 mutations specifically: ORR 44%, median PFS 10.2 months. Confirmed response duration 9.1 months (range 6.9-31.3 months). Treatment-naive patients: ORR 29%, mPFS 10.2 months. Substantially better PFS than imatinib (10.2 vs 4.2 months).

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for advanced GIST (4th line). Off-label use for KIT-mutant melanoma not yet FDA-approved but available through clinical trials and expanded access. Generic not yet available.

safety_profile.rationale: No grade 4-5 adverse events in melanoma trial. Acceptable safety profile. Most common: increased lipase (50%), alopecia (35%), actinic keratosis (19%). Better tolerated than many chemotherapy alternatives.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient has KIT exon 11 activating mutation, the most responsive subgroup to ripretinib (44% ORR for exon 11 vs 23% overall). Ripretinib has broad-spectrum KIT inhibitory activity including switch-control mechanism that may overcome imatinib resistance.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Ripretinib is a broad-spectrum switch-control tyrosine kinase inhibitor that locks KIT in an inactive conformation. Unlike imatinib, it inhibits both the activation loop and switch pocket of KIT, providing activity against primary and secondary KIT mutations including those that confer imatinib resistance.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase I Expansion Cohort - Ripretinib in KIT-Altered Melanoma

treatments.availability: FDA Approved (off-label; GIST indication); Clinical Trial for Melanoma

treatments.name: Temozolomide + Cisplatin (Adjuvant Chemotherapy)

evidence_level.rationale: Only randomized study of adjuvant chemotherapy in mucosal melanoma demonstrated significant improvement in relapse-free and overall survival. This Chinese randomized trial provides phase 3-level evidence for the adjuvant chemotherapy approach.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median relapse-free survival 20.8 months with temozolomide + cisplatin vs 9.4 months for interferon and 5.4 months for observation. Provides meaningful adjuvant benefit but less than immunotherapy approaches. Primarily studied in Chinese population.

accessibility.rationale: Both agents are generic, inexpensive, and widely available worldwide. Temozolomide is oral. Cisplatin requires infusion but is available at all oncology centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Cisplatin causes nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, peripheral neuropathy, and severe nausea. Temozolomide causes myelosuppression. Combined regimen has significant toxicity burden. Requires hydration protocols and antiemetics.

biomarker_match.rationale: Chemotherapy is universally applicable regardless of molecular profile. No biomarker requirements. May be considered if immunotherapy and targeted therapy are not tolerated or are contraindicated.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Temozolomide is an oral alkylating agent that methylates DNA at the O6 and N7 positions of guanine, causing DNA damage and cell death. Cisplatin forms platinum-DNA adducts that crosslink DNA strands, inhibiting DNA replication and transcription. The combination provides synergistic DNA damage.

key_evidence.study_name: Randomized Trial of Adjuvant Chemotherapy in Mucosal Melanoma

treatments.name: Nilotinib (Second-Generation KIT Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: TEAM trial phase II data with 42 KIT-mutant patients. More potent than imatinib in vitro against KIT. Some evidence of activity after imatinib failure.

survival_benefit.rationale: TEAM trial: ORR 26.2%, DCR 74%, median PFS 4.2 months, median OS 18.0 months. Most responders harbored exon 11 mutations. OS of 18 months compares favorably with historical chemotherapy data.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for CML. Off-label use for KIT-mutant melanoma recognized by guidelines. Generic availability improving access. Oral administration.

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized safety from CML experience. QTc prolongation requires monitoring. Hepatotoxicity possible. Generally well-tolerated with appropriate monitoring. Slightly more toxicity than imatinib.

biomarker_match.rationale: KIT exon 11 mutation predicts best response. Nilotinib is more potent than imatinib against KIT and may be effective as second-line after imatinib failure. Strong biomarker match for this patient.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Nilotinib is a second-generation BCR-ABL/KIT tyrosine kinase inhibitor approximately 20-fold more potent than imatinib against KIT in vitro. It competitively inhibits KIT ATP binding, blocking constitutive kinase activity driven by exon 11 mutations.

key_evidence.study_name: TEAM Trial - Nilotinib in KIT-Mutant Melanoma

treatments.availability: FDA Approved (off-label for melanoma; CML indication)

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab + Lenvatinib Combination

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II NeoPlus study evaluating neoadjuvant pembrolizumab + lenvatinib in resectable mucosal melanoma. Real-world evidence from French GCC group Lenvamel study. Emerging but not yet phase 3 validated for mucosal melanoma.

survival_benefit.rationale: Real-world ORR 71.4% for mucosal melanoma subgroup with lenvatinib + anti-PD-1. This is the highest ORR reported for any systemic therapy in mucosal melanoma. VEGFR inhibition may enhance immune response in the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment.

accessibility.rationale: Both agents are FDA-approved individually. The combination is not FDA-approved for mucosal melanoma but is available off-label and through clinical trials (NeoPlus study). Lenvatinib adds cost burden.

safety_profile.rationale: Combined toxicity of immunotherapy and anti-angiogenic agent. Hypertension, proteinuria, hand-foot syndrome from lenvatinib. Immune-related adverse events from pembrolizumab. Grade 3+ events in ~40-50% of patients.

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 20% supports pembrolizumab component. VEGF pathway inhibition by lenvatinib remodels tumor microenvironment to enhance immune response, potentially overcoming low TMB barrier. Not dependent on specific driver mutations.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab blocks PD-1/PD-L1 to restore anti-tumor immunity. Lenvatinib is a multi-kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR1-3, FGFR1-4, PDGFRa, RET, and KIT. Anti-angiogenic activity remodels the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment, enhancing T-cell infiltration and synergizing with checkpoint blockade.

key_evidence.journal: Journal of Clinical Oncology / The Oncologist

biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 expression favorable but not required

treatments.name: Neoadjuvant Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (Pre-Surgery)

evidence_level.rationale: Emerging approach based on retrospective studies and case reports. Neoadjuvant dual checkpoint data for sinonasal mucosal melanoma is limited. One study showed neoadjuvant ipi/nivo was not associated with better outcomes in all-comers, highlighting selection bias concerns.

survival_benefit.rationale: Potential for organ preservation if major pathologic response achieved. Case reports show complete pathologic responses. However, systematic data from Salem et al. 2025 showed neoadjuvant ipi/nivo was not consistently superior to upfront surgery for resectable sinonasal mucosal melanoma.

accessibility.rationale: Both agents FDA-approved. Neoadjuvant use is off-label but increasingly adopted at academic centers. NCCN supports consideration of neoadjuvant immunotherapy for select melanoma patients.

safety_profile.rationale: High toxicity rate with dual checkpoint blockade (55-60% Grade 3-4). Risk of delaying definitive surgery if toxicity occurs. Immune-related adverse events may complicate surgical wound healing. Treatment discontinuation in ~35-40%.

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 20% provides moderate support for checkpoint blockade response. Neoadjuvant approach may be less dependent on PD-L1 status as CTLA-4 blockade enhances T-cell priming. Applicable regardless of KIT/BRAF/NRAS status.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Neoadjuvant dual checkpoint blockade with ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) and nivolumab (anti-PD-1) aims to induce anti-tumor immune response prior to surgical resection. This approach may convert borderline-resectable tumors to resectable, enable organ preservation, and generate a systemic immune memory against micrometastatic disease.

key_evidence.study_name: Resectable Sinonasal Mucosal Melanoma: Upfront Surgery vs Neoadjuvant Therapy

biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 expression favorable but not required

treatments.name: Regorafenib (Multi-Kinase Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II study with 23 KIT-mutant melanoma patients. Responses observed across exon 11, 13, and 17 mutations. Limited sample size but promising preliminary data.

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 30.4%, median PFS 7.1 months. Responses seen across multiple KIT exon mutations. PFS compares favorably with imatinib (7.1 vs 4.2 months). Activity across mutation spectrum is an advantage.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for colorectal cancer, GIST, and HCC. Off-label use for KIT-mutant melanoma possible but not well-established. Insurance coverage may require prior authorization.

safety_profile.rationale: Known for significant toxicity profile including hand-foot skin reaction, hypertension, fatigue, diarrhea, and hepatotoxicity. Grade 3+ adverse events in approximately 50% of patients. Less well-tolerated than imatinib.

biomarker_match.rationale: KIT exon 11 mutation is among the responsive subtypes. Regorafenib has broader kinase inhibition profile than imatinib (also inhibits VEGFR, PDGFR, FGFR, RAF), which may provide additional anti-tumor activity in mucosal melanoma.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Regorafenib is an oral multi-kinase inhibitor targeting KIT, VEGFR1-3, PDGFRb, FGFR, RET, RAF, and other kinases. Its broad spectrum of kinase inhibition provides anti-angiogenic and anti-proliferative effects beyond KIT inhibition alone.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Study of Regorafenib in KIT-Mutant Melanoma

treatments.availability: FDA Approved (off-label for melanoma; CRC/GIST/HCC indications)

treatments.name: Lifileucel (TIL Adoptive Cell Therapy)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II C-144-01 data with 12 mucosal melanoma patients presented at ESMO 2023. TILVANCE-301 phase III trial ongoing for advanced melanoma including mucosal subtype. FDA-approved for cutaneous melanoma (2024).

survival_benefit.rationale: Confirmed ORR 50% in mucosal melanoma subgroup (6/12 patients). Median duration of response not reached with 4/6 responders having ongoing durable responses at data cutoff. Remarkable given mucosal melanoma low TMB (mean 2.145 mut/Mb). 12-month TIL persistence similar to cutaneous melanoma.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for cutaneous melanoma but limited availability. Only available at approximately 30 certified treatment centers in the US. Requires lymphodepletion chemotherapy, 5-7 week manufacturing time, and inpatient administration. Very high cost (~$515,000).

safety_profile.rationale: Requires lymphodepletion with cyclophosphamide and fludarabine. Cytokine release syndrome, cytopenias, and infections are significant risks. Inpatient stay of 2-3 weeks required. Treatment-related mortality risk approximately 1-2%.

biomarker_match.rationale: TIL therapy is effective regardless of PD-L1 status, TMB, or driver mutations. 50% response rate in mucosal melanoma despite low TMB demonstrates efficacy independent of traditional biomarkers. Useful for patients who have progressed on checkpoint inhibitors.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Lifileucel is an autologous tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) therapy. Tumor-reactive T cells are isolated from resected tumor tissue, expanded ex vivo to billions of cells, and infused back into the patient after lymphodepletion chemotherapy. These expanded TILs recognize tumor neoantigens and mount a direct cytotoxic response.

key_evidence.study_name: C-144-01 Phase II Trial - Mucosal Melanoma Subgroup (ESMO 2023)

treatments.availability: FDA Approved (cutaneous melanoma); Limited Specialized Centers

treatments.name: Nivolumab + Cabozantinib (Adjuvant Combination)

evidence_level.rationale: Active phase II clinical trial (NCT05111574) at UCSF. No published efficacy results yet. Rationale based on cabozantinib anti-VEGFR/MET activity and synergy with checkpoint blockade demonstrated in renal cell carcinoma.

survival_benefit.rationale: No mature survival data yet. Based on biological rationale from CheckMate-9ER (cabozantinib + nivolumab in RCC: ORR 55.7%, mPFS 16.6 months). MET and VEGFR pathways are relevant in mucosal melanoma. Potential for significant adjuvant benefit.

accessibility.rationale: Currently only available through the phase II clinical trial at UCSF. Requires eligibility screening and enrollment. Both agents individually FDA-approved but combination for mucosal melanoma is investigational.

safety_profile.rationale: Combined immune checkpoint and multi-kinase inhibitor toxicity. Cabozantinib causes hypertension, hand-foot syndrome, diarrhea, fatigue. Added to nivolumab immune-related adverse events. Grade 3+ events expected in 40-50% based on RCC data.

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 20% supports nivolumab component. Cabozantinib targets VEGFR, MET, AXL, and RET which are relevant pathways in mucosal melanoma. Not dependent on KIT/BRAF/NRAS status. Broad mechanism of action may overcome immune evasion.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Nivolumab (anti-PD-1) restores anti-tumor immunity. Cabozantinib is a multi-kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR2, MET, AXL, and RET. Cabozantinib promotes immune-permissive tumor microenvironment through VEGF pathway inhibition, enhances antigen presentation, and may overcome immunotherapy resistance through MET/AXL pathway blockade.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Trial: Nivolumab +/- Cabozantinib for Mucosal Melanoma (NCT05111574)

treatments.name: Avapritinib (Selective KIT/PDGFRa Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: Case reports and compassionate use program data only. No prospective clinical trial data in melanoma. Primarily designed for KIT exon 17/18 mutations in GIST. Limited evidence for exon 11 mutations specifically.

survival_benefit.rationale: Case reports show successful treatment responses in mucosal melanoma with KIT mutations. CNS penetration is a unique advantage. However, greatest activity seen for KIT exon 17 mutations, not exon 11 where imatinib and ripretinib are likely superior.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for GIST with PDGFRa D842V mutation. Off-label use for KIT-mutant melanoma requires compassionate use or physician-initiated protocol. Not widely prescribed for melanoma.

safety_profile.rationale: CNS effects (cognitive impairment, memory issues) are notable. Nausea, fatigue, periorbital edema. Intracranial hemorrhage risk requires monitoring. Generally manageable but requires careful oversight.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient has KIT exon 11 mutation. Avapritinib has some activity against exon 11 but is optimized for exon 17/18 activation loop mutations. May be a reserve option after imatinib/ripretinib failure if resistance mutations develop in exon 17.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Avapritinib is a highly selective, potent inhibitor of KIT and PDGFRa kinases designed to target activation loop mutations. It has excellent CNS penetration unlike imatinib, making it valuable for patients with brain metastases. Selectively inhibits KIT D816V and related activation loop variants.

key_evidence.study_name: Compassionate Use Program - Avapritinib in KIT-Mutant Mucosal Melanoma (Italy)
key_evidence.journal: European Journal of Cancer / Therapeutic Advances in Medical Oncology

biomarker_requirements: KIT activating mutation (exon 17/18 most responsive; some exon 11 activity)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (GIST); Compassionate Use for Melanoma

clinical_trials.title: Nivolumab Alone or With Cabozantinib to Prevent Mucosal Melanoma Return After Surgery
clinical_trials.early_results: No published results yet. Rationale based on cabozantinib + nivolumab synergy demonstrated in renal cell carcinoma (CheckMate-9ER).

clinical_trials.title: Phase III Trial of Lifileucel + Pembrolizumab vs Pembrolizumab Alone in Untreated Advanced Melanoma (Including Mucosal)
clinical_trials.early_results: Phase II C-144-01 showed 50% ORR in mucosal melanoma subgroup with durable responses. Median DOR not reached.

clinical_trials.title: Neoadjuvant Pembrolizumab and Lenvatinib in Resectable Mucosal Melanoma
clinical_trials.early_results: Updated at ASCO 2024: Moderate anti-tumor efficacy with pembrolizumab + lenvatinib as neoadjuvant therapy. Real-world data suggests ORR up to 71.4% in mucosal melanoma.

clinical_trials.title: Canadian Cancer Trials Group MEC6 - Immunotherapy for Mucosal Melanoma
sites: Canadian Cancer Trials Group centers, Canada
clinical_trials.early_results: No published results yet. Evaluating immunotherapy strategies specifically designed for mucosal melanoma population.

clinical_trials.title: Novel Targeted/Immunotherapy Combination for Advanced Mucosal Melanoma
clinical_trials.early_results: Newly registered trial. No results available. Evaluating novel combination strategies in molecularly selected mucosal melanoma populations.

clinical_trials.title: Expanded Access / Phase II Ripretinib in KIT-Altered Advanced Melanoma
biomarker_requirements: KIT activating mutation or KIT alteration
clinical_trials.early_results: Phase I expansion: ORR 44% in exon 11 mutations, mPFS 10.2 months. Mucosal melanoma comprised 58% of cohort. No Grade 4-5 adverse events.

combination_strategies.evidence_level: Retrospective cohort studies / Phase II data
combination_strategies.rationale: Triple-modality approach: surgery removes bulk disease, proton RT provides superior local control (88% at 1 year) with minimal late toxicity to critical structures, and adjuvant nivolumab addresses systemic micrometastatic disease. 75% of proton beam patients in Takayesu 2025 received concurrent systemic therapy. This addresses both local recurrence (primary failure mode) and distant metastasis.

Dual checkpoint blockade achieves best systemic ORR (37.1%) in mucosal melanoma. Sequential imatinib leverages the KIT exon 11 mutation as an additional therapeutic axis. KIT inhibition may be added after immunotherapy completion or at progression. Different mechanisms of action provide non-cross-resistant sequential therapy.

Lenvatinib remodels the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment through VEGF pathway inhibition, enhancing T-cell infiltration and synergizing with PD-1 blockade. Real-world ORR 71.4% in mucosal melanoma represents the highest reported response rate. VEGFR inhibition may also contribute KIT pathway suppression relevant to this patient's mutation.

Imatinib as first-line KIT-targeted therapy (ORR 54% for KIT-mutant) followed by ripretinib upon progression. Ripretinib's switch-control mechanism inhibits secondary KIT resistance mutations that develop on imatinib. Prior KIT inhibitor-naive patients on ripretinib achieve ORR 29% and mPFS 10.2 months. Ensures continuous KIT pathway suppression through resistance evolution.

The only randomized trial of adjuvant chemotherapy in mucosal melanoma showed median RFS of 20.8 months with temozolomide/cisplatin versus 5.4 months for observation. This combination may be considered when immunotherapy is contraindicated or as consolidation after surgery and radiation, particularly in settings where immunotherapy access is limited.

supportive_care.approach: Structured Exercise and Physical Rehabilitation Program
supportive_care.evidence: Meta-analyses demonstrate exercise reduces cancer-related fatigue and improves quality of life during and after melanoma treatment. Supervised adapted physical activity programs are safe and well-tolerated with no adverse events in advanced melanoma patients.
supportive_care.benefit: First-line treatment for cancer-related fatigue. Improves physical function, quality of life, and psychological well-being. May enhance treatment tolerance and immune function. Feasible even during systemic therapy.

supportive_care.evidence: Nutritional counseling with dietitian support maintains lean body mass, supports immune function during immunotherapy, and prevents treatment-related weight loss. Head and neck cancer patients are at high risk for nutritional compromise due to sinonasal location affecting eating.
supportive_care.benefit: Maintains treatment tolerance, supports immune reconstitution during checkpoint inhibitor therapy, prevents sarcopenia which is associated with worse immunotherapy outcomes. Protein-rich diet supports wound healing post-surgery.

supportive_care.evidence: Systematic review demonstrates patient-centered psychological support, education, and behavioral change interventions improve melanoma survivorship outcomes. Young patients (age 45) with rare cancers face unique psychosocial challenges.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduces anxiety and depression, improves treatment adherence, enhances quality of life. Addresses existential distress associated with rare aggressive cancer diagnosis. Supports decision-making for complex treatment options.

supportive_care.evidence: Sinonasal surgery and radiation cause chronic nasal crusting, dryness, and impaired mucociliary clearance. Regular nasal saline irrigations, topical treatments, and ENT follow-up reduce complications and improve quality of life.
supportive_care.benefit: Maintains nasal airway patency, reduces crusting-related discomfort, prevents sinusitis, monitors for local recurrence. Saline irrigations improve mucosal healing after radiation therapy.

supportive_care.evidence: Standardized immune-related adverse event (irAE) monitoring protocols with baseline labs (thyroid function, liver function, cortisol) and regular monitoring reduce severe toxicity. Early corticosteroid intervention prevents irAE escalation.
supportive_care.benefit: Enables early detection and management of immune-related adverse events, prevents treatment interruptions, maintains treatment intensity and duration. Multidisciplinary irAE management teams improve outcomes.

supportive_care.approach: Dermatologic Sun Protection and Skin Surveillance
supportive_care.evidence: While sinonasal mucosal melanoma is not UV-related, comprehensive skin surveillance detects second primary melanomas. Mucosal melanoma patients have elevated risk of additional melanocytic neoplasms.
supportive_care.benefit: Early detection of second primaries or cutaneous metastases. Regular dermatologic surveillance with dermoscopy. Full-body skin exam every 6-12 months recommended.
